understandingTheDifferenceBetw: Understanding the difference between fin seal and lap seal is crucial for optimizing your packaging's performance, cost, and appearance. This guide covers everything you need to make the right choice.
joinsTwoInnerSealantLayersToge: Joins two inner sealant layers together, creating a raised "fin" seam along the back.
strongerSealMoreMaterialUseIde: Stronger seal, more material use, ideal for high-barrier applications.
overlapsOneEdgeOverAnotherSeal: Overlaps one edge over another, sealing inner to outer surface.
flatterAppearance1015LessMater: Flatter appearance, 10-15% less material, better for branding but slightly weaker.
joinsTheTwoInnerSealantLayersO: joins the two inner (sealant) layers of the film together, creating a raised "fin" seam that usually runs vertically along the back of the package.
airtightMoistureResistantSeamI: Airtight, moisture-resistant seam ideal for perishable foods, pharmaceuticals, and products needing long shelf life.
toleratesHigherInternalPressur: Tolerates higher internal pressure, heavier products, and rough handling during transport.
worksWithFilmsSealableOnOneSid: Works with films sealable on one side only (e.g., PET/PE laminates), common in high-barrier structures.
overlapsOneFilmEdgeOverTheOthe: overlaps one film edge over the other and seals the inner surface of one edge to the outer surface of the other, forming a flatter, smoother seam.
usesSmallerOverlapSavingApprox: Uses smaller overlap, saving approximately 10-15% film versus fin seals for the same bag size.
flatSmoothSeamWithUninterrupte: Flat, smooth seam with uninterrupted back-panel graphics. Often preferred for branding and shelf appeal.
commonOnHighVolumeSnackLinesWh: Common on high-volume snack lines where cost and appearance matter more than maximum barrier.
productNeedsStrongBarrierAndSe: Product needs strong barrier and seal integrity (oxygen/moisture sensitive)
filmHasASealableLayerOnlyOnThe: Film has a sealable layer only on the inside (high-barrier laminates)
packagingHeavyOrOilyProductsTh: Packaging heavy or oily products that stress seals
longShelfLifeIsCriticalCoffeeP: Long shelf life is critical (coffee, powders, pet food, pharma)
reducingFilmCostAndImprovingSu: Reducing film cost and improving sustainability is a priority
youWantTheFlattestCleanestBack: You want the flattest, cleanest back panel for branding
productsAreLightAndDryChipsCan: Products are light and dry (chips, candy, crackers)
runningHighVolumeLinesWhereCos: Running high-volume lines where cost per unit matters most
lapSealsUseSmallerOverlapWhile: Lap seals use smaller overlap while fin seals need extra 12-20mm film width per pack.
theHigherYourYearlyFilmUsageTh: The higher your yearly film usage, the more impactful that 10-15% reduction becomes.
ifLapSealsLeadToMoreLeaksForYo: If lap seals lead to more leaks for your product, waste costs can offset material savings.
